summary: 19 concrete mentions surfaced across 21 qualifying stories.

items.name: Zometool
items.summary: Modular construction system praised for architecture workshops and creative builds.
items.why_included: A commenter explicitly endorsed it as a fun, effective modular system.
items.evidence: “Zometool rules!” and “I run workshops on modular systems in architecture using Zometool”

items.name: Mikrotik 10 GbE switches
items.source_story_title: New 10 GbE USB adapters are cooler, smaller, cheaper
items.summary: Affordable 10GbE switch options praised as a good-value upgrade.
items.why_included: A commenter pointed to concrete Mikrotik hardware as a cheap path to 10GbE.
items.evidence: “10 Gb is cheap! Mikrotik has a 4x10Gb + 1x1Gb port switch for $150 USD and an 8x10Gb version for about $275.”

items.name: The Naked Ape
items.summary: Desmond Morris anthropology book called a seminal work.
items.why_included: The thread explicitly praised it as an important book.

items.name: Catwatching
items.summary: Desmond Morris cat-observation book praised as thoughtful.
items.why_included: A commenter recommended it as a concrete Morris title worth reading.
items.evidence: “I know him from his book "Catwatching", which is a very thoughtful observational study of cats.”

items.name: Godot
items.source_story_title: Using coding assistance tools to revive projects you never were going to finish
items.summary: Open-source game engine mentioned as a fun revival target.
items.why_included: The thread framed it as a productive, enjoyable pairing with coding assistance.
items.evidence: “Claude Code + Godot = fun. Going back to it.”

items.name: Deepsid / Chordian SID player
items.source_story_title: Martin Galway's music source files from 1980's Commodore 64 games
items.summary: Browser SID player shared for listening to Wizball tunes directly.
items.why_included: A concrete listening tool was directly shared in the comments.
items.evidence: “You can listen to the Wizball tunes here: https://deepsid.chordian.net/?file=/MUSICIANS/G/Galway_Martin/Wizball.sid&subtune=4”

items.name: Slayradio
items.source_story_title: Martin Galway's music source files from 1980's Commodore 64 games
items.summary: C64 music radio site recommended as a longtime favorite.
items.why_included: A commenter described it as a long-term listening staple.
items.evidence: “I’ve been listening this on and off for more than a decade: https://slayradio.org/”

items.name: Kloak
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Kloak, A secret manager that keeps K8s workload away from secrets
items.summary: Kubernetes secret manager that rewrites placeholders at runtime.
items.why_included: A commenter clearly endorsed the idea and wished the author luck.
items.evidence: “Idea is good though, wish you luck.”

items.name: Hokusai Moyo Gafu
items.summary: Hokusai pattern book shared as a concrete tessellation source.
items.why_included: The thread surfaced an actual book copy and accessible scans.
items.evidence: “Found a copy of the book on Wikimedia.” and “the page has a British Museum version with an English interface.”

items.name: Embassy
items.source_story_title: What async promised and what it delivered
items.summary: Embedded Rust async framework recommended for safe concurrent code.
items.why_included: Commenters framed it as a strong fit for embedded async work.
items.evidence: “In embedded Rust, async is seen as especially strong when paired with frameworks like Embassy or RTIC.”

items.name: RTIC
items.source_story_title: What async promised and what it delivered
items.summary: Real-time concurrency framework recommended alongside Embassy.
items.why_included: The thread explicitly grouped it with Embassy as a recommended embedded framework.
items.evidence: “In embedded Rust, async is seen as especially strong when paired with frameworks like Embassy or RTIC.”

items.name: Azure Bastion
items.source_story_title: A web-based RDP client built with Go WebAssembly and grdp
items.summary: Managed browser-based RDP/SSH bastion praised for file and clipboard support.
items.why_included: Commenters used it as a real-world benchmark for browser admin UX.
items.evidence: Azure Bastion was praised as handling files and clipboards well and supporting console sessions.

items.name: Google Cloud SSH-in-browser
items.source_story_title: A web-based RDP client built with Go WebAssembly and grdp
items.summary: Google Cloud’s in-browser SSH access for Compute Engine VMs.
items.why_included: The thread praised it as a top-tier browser-based admin experience.
items.evidence: GCP’s browser SSH was described as the best seen so far.

items.name: Conventional Comments
items.summary: PR comment convention for marking blocking and non-blocking feedback.
items.why_included: A commenter recommended it as a practical way to label review comments.
items.evidence: “I make it clear what is non-blocking/blocking with Conventional Comments: https://conventionalcomments.org”

items.name: Beancount
items.source_story_title: Plain text has been around for decades and it’s here to stay
items.summary: Plain-text accounting system praised as a QuickBooks replacement.
items.why_included: A commenter described using it in a real accounting workflow.
items.evidence: “replacing QuickBooks with Beancount + Fava for a sole proprietorship”

items.name: Fava
items.source_story_title: Plain text has been around for decades and it’s here to stay
items.summary: Web UI for Beancount’s plain-text accounting workflow.
items.why_included: The thread praised it as part of the QuickBooks replacement setup.
items.evidence: “replacing QuickBooks with Beancount + Fava for a sole proprietorship; no ads, commit history proving when entries were made”

items.name: Omarchy
items.source_story_title: Plain text has been around for decades and it’s here to stay
items.summary: Opinionated Linux desktop stack cited as a TUI renaissance example.
items.why_included: Commenters held it up as evidence that TUIs are having a moment.
items.evidence: Some argued the peak of TUIs is now, citing projects like Omarchy.

items.name: Zed Ask mode
items.summary: Read-only AI mode for querying code and getting help without editing.
items.why_included: A commenter described it as the right way to keep the human in charge.
items.evidence: “example: Zed ‘ask’ mode useful as a rubber duck and can read code directly”

items.name: Niri
items.summary: Scrollable-tiling Wayland compositor praised for ultrawide workflows.
items.why_included: The thread was overwhelmingly positive about its desktop ergonomics.
items.evidence: Commenters said Niri is “so good” and especially strong on ultrawides.
